figures.caption: Figure 10: Example visualization used for fMRI-to-T1w co-registration QC. The red contours correspond to T1w-derived anatomical boundaries overlaid on the co-registered mean BOLD image.

Table 5: Detailed ablation records on ADHD-200. Runtime is reported in hours. Prompt and completion tokens are abbreviated in M/K units. TO denotes timeout. N/A indicates this run failed completely, so the timing statistics are not meaningful.

Table 2: Summary statistics across five independent runs of NIAgent. Values are reported as mean (range) across runs. Completion denotes producing all required deliverables; adaptive modeling refinement denotes downstream-analysis iterations triggered by runtime observations such as validation performance, feature instability, or overfitting risk.
Table 10: Quality control primitives used in NIAgent.
Figure 11: Example visualization used for fMRI-to-MNI normalization QC. The red contours correspond to MNI template boundaries overlaid on the normalized mean BOLD image.
Table 6: Detailed ablation records on ADNI. Runtime is reported in hours. Prompt and completion tokens are abbreviated in M/K units. TO denotes timeout.
Figure 5: Example visualization used for raw T1w visual QC. The figure is a mosaic view from the MRIQC T1-weighted report, focused on the brain parenchyma.
Figure 1: Overview of the NIAgent framework.
Figure 6: Example visualization used for T1w skull-stripping QC. The red contour shows the extracted brain mask.
Table 4: Example running trajectory of NIAgent on ADNI from one of five runs.

Table 7: Number of subjects retained after quality control across the five independent end-to-end runs of NIAgent. For each run, we report the original cohort size, the number of subjects passing raw-data QC, and the number of subjects passing post-processing QC. Here, post-processing QC refers to quality control applied to the outputs of the developed preprocessing pipeline (e.g., spatial normalization, registration, skull stripping, or tissue segmentation, depending on the dataset and workflow). Only subjects passing post-processing QC are retained for downstream analysis and final model training.
Table 11: Processing primitives used in NIAgent.
Figure 7: Example visualization used for T1w tissue-segmentation QC. Red indicates the brain mask, blue indicates the GM/WM boundary, and green indicates the tissue/CSF boundary.
Table 3: Example running trajectory of NIAgent on ADHD-200 from one of five runs.
Table 12: Downstream analysis primitives used in NIAgent.

Figure 4: Example questionnaire page used for human evaluation in the QC agreement study, shown here for the skull_strip_qc checkpoint. Each sampled case was presented to raters for independent binary judgment (PASS or FAIL).
Figure 3: Evaluation of the closed loop autonomous QC module.
Figure 8: Example visualization used for T1w-to-MNI normalization QC. The red outlines correspond to the MNI template contours overlaid on the subject’s normalized T1w image.
Figure 9: Example visualization used for raw fMRI visual QC. The figure shows the MRIQC mosaic of the mean BOLD signal.
